note: First bodyBlock is the <aside> system-note. ALL point figures kept 1:1; only labels/headers/round names translated.

fields.title: ATP Ranking Points Explained — 2026 Point Distribution | SUPER.TENNIS
fields.metaDescription: ATP ranking points 2026: full point distribution tables for Grand Slams, Masters 1000 (96-draw and 56-draw), ATP 500, ATP 250, ATP Finals, and Challengers. How the best-19 rule works and how points actually expire.
fields.quickAnswer: ATP rankings are based on a player's best 19 results over a 52-week rolling window. Grand Slam champions earn 2,000 points; Masters 1000 winners earn 1,000; ATP 500 winners earn 500; ATP 250 winners earn 250. The ATP Finals year-end championship can award up to 1,500 points to an undefeated champion. Players must play the mandatory events (all 4 Grand Slams + the qualifying Masters 1000 set), or those slots count as zeros. The 2024 ATP point restructure expanded most Masters 1000 events to 96-draw / 12-day formats, with slightly stretched point tables for those tournaments.
bodyBlocks:
Current System Note

Point tables on this page reflect the 2024 ATP point restructure, still in effect for the 2026 season. Five Masters 1000 events now use 96-draw / 12-day formats with slightly stretched point tables; the other four keep the older 56-draw / 1-week format.
Grand Slam Points (W: 2,000)
All four Grand Slams (Australian Open, Roland Garros, Wimbledon, US Open) award identical points. The men's singles draw is 128 players (best-of-five sets — see match format rules).

Round ReachedPoints
Champion (W)2,000
Final (F)1,300
Semifinal (SF)800
Quarterfinal (QF)400
Round of 16 (R16)200
Round of 32 (R32)100
Round of 64 (R64)50
Round of 128 (R128, first-round win)10
Masters 1000 — 96-Draw Events (W: 1,000)
Used at Indian Wells, Miami Open, Madrid Open, Italian Open (Rome), and Shanghai Masters. These events run 12 days and use 96-player draws (32 seeds get a first-round bye).

Round ReachedPoints
Champion (W)1,000
Final (F)650
Semifinal (SF)400
Quarterfinal (QF)200
Round of 16 (R16)100
Round of 32 (R32)50
Round of 64 (R64)30
Round of 128 (R128, first-round win)10
Masters 1000 — 56-Draw Events (W: 1,000)
Used at Monte-Carlo, Canadian Open, Cincinnati Open, and Paris Masters. These events run 1 week with 56-player draws (top 8 seeds get first-round byes).

Round ReachedPoints
Champion (W)1,000
Final (F)650
Semifinal (SF)400
Quarterfinal (QF)200
Round of 16 (R16)100
Round of 32 (R32)50
Examples: Barcelona Open, Halle Open, Queen's Club, Vienna Open, Tokyo, Rotterdam, Dubai, Rio Open, Acapulco.

Round ReachedPoints
Champion (W)500
Final (F)330
Semifinal (SF)200
Quarterfinal (QF)100
Round of 16 (R16)50
Round of 32 (R32)25
The smallest tour tier, with about 30 events worldwide. Useful for top players to build form, and the main path for rising players to enter the top 100.

Round ReachedPoints
Champion (W)250
Final (F)165
Semifinal (SF)100
Quarterfinal (QF)50
Round of 16 (R16)25
ATP Finals (Up to 1,500)

The ATP Finals (held in Turin through 2025, then moving venues) is the year-end championship for the top 8 ranked players. Points are cumulative: a player earns 200 for each round-robin match win, plus 400 for the semifinal win, plus 500 for the final win.

AchievementPoints
Round-robin match win200 each (up to 3 wins = 600)
Semifinal win400
Final win500
Maximum (undefeated champion)1,500
The Best-19 Rule
A player's ATP ranking is the sum of their best 19 results over the past 52 weeks. The 19 slots are filled in this priority order:

1. All 4 Grand Slams (whether or not the player participated)
  2. The qualifying Masters 1000 events (per the player's mandatory list — typically 8 of the 9)
  3. ATP Finals (if the player qualified)
  4. The player's best remaining results from any other counting events (ATP 500, 250, Challengers, Olympics, Davis Cup)

Skipping a mandatory event puts a zero in that slot. Skipping a non-mandatory tournament doesn't penalize the player at all — they simply earn 0 from that event and might fill the slot with a better result elsewhere.
Worked Example
Imagine an ATP top-20 player ending the season with the following best results:

• Wimbledon SF — 800 points
  • Australian Open QF — 400
  • Roland Garros R16 — 200
  • US Open R32 — 100
  • Indian Wells F — 650
  • Miami SF — 400
  • Rome QF — 200
  • Madrid R16 — 100
  • Shanghai R32 — 50
  • Monte Carlo SF — 400
  • Canada QF — 200
  • Cincinnati R16 — 100
  • Paris R32 — 50
  • Queen's W — 500
  • Dubai SF — 200
  • Halle QF — 100
  • Vienna W — 500
  • Tokyo F — 330
  • Rotterdam SF — 200
Total: 5,580 points, roughly top-10. The takeaway: deep Slam runs (800 for a SF) and Masters 1000 wins are the foundation; ATP 500 wins fill the remaining slots.

faqs.q: How many points does a Grand Slam winner get in ATP rankings?
faqs.a: The men's singles champion at any of the four Grand Slams earns 2,000 ATP ranking points. The runner-up earns 1,300. Semifinalists earn 800 each, quarterfinalists 400, round-of-16 200, round-of-32 100, round-of-64 50, and any player who wins their first-round match (advancing from the round of 128) earns 10 points.

faqs.q: What is the ATP best-19 rule?
faqs.a: The ATP rankings only count a player's best 19 tournament results over the rolling 52-week period. Within those 19, the mandatory events (4 Grand Slams + 8-9 qualifying Masters 1000 events + ATP Finals if the player qualifies) always count. The remaining slots go to the player's best results from other ATP events (500s, 250s, Olympic medal, Davis Cup, Challengers).

faqs.q: What happens if an ATP player skips a mandatory event?
faqs.a: Skipping a mandatory event counts as a zero-point result in the player's best-19. The previous year's points at that tournament expire and are not replaced. Top-100 players who repeatedly skip mandatory events (without approved medical exemption) face fines and tour-disciplinary measures, in addition to the ranking drop.

faqs.q: How much can a player earn at the ATP Finals?
faqs.a: At the ATP Finals (year-end championship for the top 8), each round-robin match win earns 200 points. The semifinal winner gets an additional 400 points, and the final winner gets another 500. An undefeated champion (3 round-robin wins + SF + F) earns the maximum 1,500 points. A champion who lost a round-robin match but still made the final can still earn up to 1,500 by winning the title.

faqs.q: Do Challenger and ITF event points count toward ATP rankings?
faqs.a: Yes. ATP Challenger Tour events (Challenger 50/75/100/125/175) and the highest-tier ITF Futures awards points that count toward the ATP world ranking. They fill the lower slots in the best-19 system once mandatory and main tour events are accounted for. Challenger 175 winners earn 175 points; Challenger 100 winners earn 100; smaller ones earn less.

faqs.q: Does the Olympics give ATP ranking points?
faqs.a: From 2024 onwards, Olympic singles medals award ranking points: 400 for gold, 280 for silver, 180 for bronze, plus smaller amounts for earlier rounds. From 2008 to 2020 the Olympics gave variable points (and zero in 2024 before being reinstated). The exact 2026 point structure for Olympic tennis is confirmed for LA28 — check the ATP for any in-cycle adjustments.

faqs.q: How many Masters 1000 events are there in 2026?
faqs.a: There are 9 ATP Masters 1000 events: Indian Wells, Miami Open, Monte-Carlo Masters, Madrid Open, Italian Open (Rome), Canadian Open (Toronto/Montreal), Cincinnati Open, Shanghai Masters, and Paris Masters. Five of them (Indian Wells, Miami, Madrid, Rome, Shanghai) use 96-player draws spanning 12 days; the other four use the older 56-draw / 1-week format.
